The temperate deciduous forest and residential building materials are nearly identical to the Northern US, particularly in Ontario or Quebec. Geographically, the terrain and vegetation provide no distinguishing feature to separate it from the US border regions.

Canada would show similar vegetation, but the specific mix of residential landscaping and forest density is very common in the Appalachian/New England region.

The temperate deciduous forest and ornamental landscaping are consistent with Southern Ontario or Quebec. It is botanically indistinguishable from the Northern US in this specific suburban/rural interface.

The wooden utility pole design and the style of the residential driveway/garage are highly characteristic of North American suburban infrastructure. The road is a narrow paved lane without markings, common in rural US residential areas.

The wooden utility pole with a simple top-mounted wire attachment and the specific style of residential architecture (stone veneer combined with horizontal siding and a wide garage door) are highly characteristic of North American suburbs. The lack of a shoulder and the specific texture of the asphalt are common in rural US residential areas.

Canada shares nearly identical utility pole designs and residential construction materials (stone and siding) with the US. It is distinguished from the US only by subtle differences in regional building codes not visible here.
